coredata.dc:title: Enhancing plant photosynthesis with dual light conversion films incorporating biomass-derived carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Institution of Chemical Engineers (IChemE).

coredata.dc:creator: Ge, Min
Yuan, Yue
Liu, Shouxin
Li, Jian
Yang, Chenhui
Du, Baosheng
Pang, Qiuying
Li, Shujun
Chen, Zhijun
coredata.dc:description: Enhancing photosynthesis is a pivotal strategy for achieving sustainable plant production. Blue and red light facilitate plant growth since these wavelengths are readily absorbed by chlorophyll pigments and power crucial photosynthetic processes. In this investigation, double light conversion films were prepared by incorporating biomass-derived carbon dots into a polyvinyl alcohol matrix (CDs@PVAs). The study conclusively demonstrated that CDs@PVAs can convert ultraviolet and green light from sunlight into blue and red light. Using 2-week-old Athaliana plants as the model organism, the Athaliana plants were covered with CDs@PVAs and then exposed to simulated sunlight (0.57 mW cm−2) for 1 hour. The Fv/Fm value in the presence of the CDs@PVAs was approximately 12% higher than without the film, indicating a significant boost in photosynthesis. Analysis of gene expression showed that the CDs@PVAs cause significant upregulation of genes associated with photosynthesis. These double light conversion films thus emerge as promising contenders for eco-friendly plant cultivation methods that circumvent reliance on electric power. Their potential applications in agriculture are substantial, underscoring their significance in promoting sustainable practices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Light conversion films
Biomass
Carbon dots
Plant photosynthesis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanism by which CDs@PVA films promote Athaliana photosynthesis

GZ20220115), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Grant No. 2021M690572), the Heilongjiang Provincial Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Grant No. LBHZ21033), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Grant No. 2572022BB05).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic showing preparation and application of light conversion films.

Morphology and structure of CDs. (a) TEM image of CDs (inset: HRTEM image of CDs). (b) Size distribution of CDs. (c) Raman spectrum of CDs. (d) XRD spectrum of powdered CDs. (e) FT-IR spectrum of CDs. (f) Full XPS spectrum of CDs. (g-i) High resolution C1s, O1s and N1s XPS spectra of CDs.

Optical properties of chloroplasts, CDs and chloroplast/CD complexes. (a) UV–vis spectrum of methanolic solution of CDs (20 μg mL−1). (b) Absorption spectrum of chloroplasts and emission spectra of CDs at different concentrations (Ex = 320 nm, 20 μg mL−1 and Ex = 510 nm, 200 μg mL−1). (c) Schematic showing enhancement of Athaliana photosynthesis by aqueous solution of CDs (d) Photographs of Athaliana sprayed with different concentrations of CDs and grown in sunlight for 5 days with corresponding chlorophyll fluorescence images. (e) Chlorophyll fluorescence parameter Fv/Fm of Athaliana after 3 and 5 days of growth. (f) Fresh weight of Athaliana 5 days after spraying with CDs.

CDs@PVAs as light conversion films. (a) Absorption spectrum of chloroplasts and emission spectra (Em) of 20-CDs@PVA and 200-CDs@PVA (Ex = 320 nm and 510 nm). (b) Schematic showing enhancement of chloroplast photosynthesis by double-layer films. (c) DCPIP reduction by chloroplasts covered with PVA and CDs@PVA films under simulated sunlight (20 mW cm−2). (d) Rate of photosynthesis in the absence and presence of PVA and CDs@PVA films. (e) Images showing Athaliana plants and fluorescence of Athaliana chlorophyll (scale bar = 3 cm). (f) Chlorophyll fluorescence parameter Fv/Fm of Athaliana chlorophyll in the absence and presence of double-layer CDs@PVA films.
Pathway enrichment analysis in Athaliana covered with light conversion films. (a) GO term classification of responsive genes in Athaliana. (b) KEGG pathway enrichment analysis.
Summary of photophysical properties of methanolic solutions of CDs with different concentrations.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: CIE = Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage color space.

head.ce:title: Enhancing plant photosynthesis with dual light conversion films incorporating biomass-derived carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science and Technology of Ministry of Education, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin, 150040, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Laser Propulsion and Application, Space Engineering University, Beijing, 101416, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Laser Propulsion and Application

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cState Key Laboratory of Laser Propulsion and Application, Space Engineering University, Beijing, 101416, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Enhancing photosynthesis is a pivotal strategy for achieving sustainable plant production. Blue and red light facilitate plant growth since these wavelengths are readily absorbed by chlorophyll pigments and power crucial photosynthetic processes. In this investigation, double light conversion films were prepared by incorporating biomass-derived carbon dots into a polyvinyl alcohol matrix (CDs@PVAs). The study conclusively demonstrated that CDs@PVAs can convert ultraviolet and green light from sunlight into blue and red light. Using 2-week-old Athaliana plants as the model organism, the Athaliana plants were covered with CDs@PVAs and then exposed to simulated sunlight (0.57 mW cm−2) for 1 hour. The Fv/Fm value in the presence of the CDs@PVAs was approximately 12% higher than without the film, indicating a significant boost in photosynthesis. Analysis of gene expression showed that the CDs@PVAs cause significant upregulation of genes associated with photosynthesis. These double light conversion films thus emerge as promising contenders for eco-friendly plant cultivation methods that circumvent reliance on electric power. Their potential applications in agriculture are substantial, underscoring their significance in promoting sustainable practices.

The innovative dual-conversion films, which integrate PVA and biomass-derived carbon dots synthesized from natural furfural, significantly enhance light-harvesting efficiency and improve the overall photosynthetic performance in plants.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:section.ce:para: Boosting plant growth and biomass production by improved harvesting of solar energy for photosynthesis and mitigating the harmful effects of ozone-depleting substances in the atmosphere are two important goals (Blankenship et al., 2011; Ince et al., 2022; Li et al., 2021b; Lin et al., 2020; 1931; Wang et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2022). Sunlight's blue, red, and far-red wavelengths traditionally play a pivotal role in plant development compared to ultraviolet (UV) or green light (Han et al., 2024; Ho et al., 2016; Xu et al., 2022). Converting UV and green light into wavelengths usable by plants would not only mitigate the harmful effects of UV light but also stimulate plant growth since plants can typically use less than 10% of sunlight for photosynthesis (Huang et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2017; Zhu et al., 2010). The conversion of UV and green light into blue and red light would enhance the photosynthetic efficiency of plants and thus address a pressing need in sustainable plant growth initiatives.

Biomass-based carbon dots (CDs), characterized by tunable fluorescence, low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, and sustainability, have emerged as a promising class of fluorescent nanoparticles (Chen et al., 2022; Kumar and Rhim, 2024; Li, R. et al.; Yang et al., 2024; Zhang, H. et al., 2024). These unique advantages set CDs apart from traditional quantum dots (QDs) and organic dyes (Hu et al., 2023; Wang, Y. et al., 2021), positioning them as promising candidates for a wide range of applications, including biosensors (He et al., 2024, 2023; Krasley et al., 2024; Zhang, X. et al., 2024), optical imaging (Guo et al., 2024; Nong et al., 2024), and biomedicine (Krasley et al., 2024; Li et al., 2018). Several studies have explored the uptake, translocation and phytotoxicity of CDs in plants, as well as their effect on photosynthesis, primarily via the root absorption pathway, using mung beans, lettuce and maize (Li et al., 2021a; Wang et al., 2021; Wang, H. et al., 2018; Xu et al., 2020), suggesting their potential as live imaging markers in plant systems (Lin et al., 2023). There are, however, few reports describing the safety and effectiveness of applying CDs or CDs-containing films as light-converting materials to plant leaves to enhance photosynthetic efficiency, while maximizing the utilization of solar energy across the full spectrum.

In this study, we have successfully demonstrated acceleration of plant growth using double light conversion films, prepared by incorporating biomass-based CDs into a polyvinyl alcohol matrix (CDs@PVAs) (Scheme 1 ). CDs emit blue light at lower concentrations and red light at higher concentrations, both wavelengths overlapping with chloroplast absorption spectra. This alignment enhances chloroplast light-harvesting efficiency, thereby boosting photosynthetic efficiency. By adjusting the concentration of CDs in the PVA matrix, these innovative films effectively convert UV and green light from sunlight into blue and red light, enhancing plant light absorption capability. The CDs@PVAs were shown experimentally to enhance the overall rate of photosynthesis by contributing to an increased rate of electron transfer in the photosynthetic process. Analysis of gene expression revealed that the CDs@PVAs caused a marked upregulation in the expression of genes associated with photosynthesis. These dual light conversion films thus show remarkable promise for sustainable, eco-friendly agricultural plant cultivation.

The CDs were synthesized from furfural and p-phenylenediamine using the hydrothermal method. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images showed that the CDs are monodisperse, spherical nanoparticles (Fig. 1 a and Fig. S1). The majority of the nanoparticles have an average diameter of 1.9 nm (Fig. 1b). The HRTEM image of the CDs showed a lattice fringe of 0.24 nm (Fig. 1a, inset), which corresponds to the (101) interplanar spacing of graphitic carbon and confirms that the CDs have a graphite-like carbon structure (Chang et al., 2022). The low intensity ratio (ID/IG = 0.87) of the distinct D and G bands of graphitic carbon at ∼1340 cm−1 and ∼1580 cm−1, respectively (Wang et al., 2023), in the Raman spectrum provided further evidence that the CDs are highly graphitized, likely due to formation of a graphitized core during the carbonization process (Fig. 1c). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the CDs showed a broad peak centered at 20°, which was assigned to an amorphous carbon phase, suggesting that the CDs contain not only graphite-like carbon but also have multiple surface states (Fig. 1d) (Ge et al., 2021b). The Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum of the CDs (Fig. 1e) confirmed the presence of abundant functional groups. In particular, signals were observed at 3420–3150 cm−1 and 1615 cm−1, corresponding to –OH/–NH2 and –C = N groups, respectively (Ge et al., 2021a; Li et al., 2024). Analysis of the chemical composition of the CDs by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) indicated the presence of C (60.79%), O (25.53%) and N (13.68%) (Fig. 1f). The C1s spectrum (Fig. 1g) showed three main bands at 284.3 eV (C–C/C = C), 285.5 eV (C–O/C–N) and 287.4 eV (C = O) (Feng et al., 2024; Gong et al., 2024); the O1s spectrum (Fig. 1h) showed three bands at 530.2 eV (C–O), 531.5 eV (C = O) and 533.5 eV (C–O–C and C–O–H) (Chen et al., 2023; Li, J. et al.); and the N1s spectrum (Fig. 1i) showed three bands at 399.0 eV (N = C), 400.0 eV (N–C) and 402.1 eV (N–H), further confirming the presence of numerous functional groups on the CDs (Guo et al., 2024). This comprehensive analysis thus indicated that a graphitic carbon core was formed during the hydrothermal process and that the CDs contain numerous functional groups (Fig. S2).

Numerous previous studies have demonstrated that the PL of CDs originates from a combination of core states and surface-related states (Li et al., 2023; Pan et al., 2010); the competition between surface and internal states gives rise to the phenomenon of double emission (Zhang et al., 2019). The mechanism behind this phenomenon can be elucidated by analysis of cyclic voltammetry curves and UV absorption spectra, which allows calculation of the energy levels of the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of the CDs. The optical band gap of the CDs, deduced from the UV absorption spectrum, was approximately 1.63 eV (Fig. S5a and b) and the reduction potential of the CDs was calculated from the cyclic voltammetry curves to be 0.18 eV (Fig. S5c). Based on Equations (S1) and (S2), the energy levels of the LUMO and HOMO of the CDs were calculated to be −4.58 and −6.21 eV, respectively. The excitation spectra of the CDs, measured at concentrations of 20 and 200 μg mL−1, showed distinct peaks at 320 nm and 510 nm, corresponding to electronic transitions of 3.88 eV and 2.43 eV, respectively. These transitions signify movements from the HOMO to the LUMO and are aligned with the π→π* transition of the aromatic ring and the n→π* transition of the conjugate N (Fig. S5d). The primary peak, situated around 450 nm (2.76 eV), thus indicates the excited state of the monodisperse carbon core, while the subsequent peak represents the excited state of aggregated surface defect states. Notably, for the triplet carbene structure, the energy difference (∆E) between the σ and π orbitals should fall below 1.5 eV (Wang, L. et al., 2018). In our investigation, ∆E was calculated to be 1.45 eV, confirming the validity of the emission mechanism within the required range for the free carbene. This suggests that functional groups can modulate the emission spectra of CDs, influencing their ability to convert ultraviolet and green light into blue and red light usable by chloroplasts. A plausible mechanism for the double emission phenomenon in CDs is provided in Fig. S5d.

When excited, methanolic solutions of CDs with concentrations of 20 μg mL−1 and 200 μg mL−1 can emit blue and red light, which overlap with the absorption wavelength of chloroplasts (Fig. 2b). This indicates that the light-harvesting capability of the CDs has the potential to enhance plant photosynthesis efficiency (Xiao et al., 2021). Before exploring potential applications of CDs, we investigated their photostability. The intensity of the fluorescence emission of CDs at concentrations of 20 μg mL−1 and 200 μg mL−1 showed minimal reduction, after 50 min of excitation at 365 nm and 500 nm (Fig. S6a and 6b), demonstrating that CDs are highly resistant to photobleaching. Therefore, we proceeded to investigate the photosynthetic activity of chloroplast/CDs complexes by measuring the rate of reduction of 2,6-dichlorophenol-indophenol (DCPIP). In this context, the functional groups on CDs may enhance their affinity for chloroplasts, facilitating their integration and subsequent light-harvesting capability. In the Hill reaction, isolated chloroplasts decompose water under light irradiation to release oxygen and reduce electron acceptors. DCPIP has a strong affinity for electrons transferred from photosystem II (PS II) to PS I during the photoreaction stage and can participate in the reaction as an electron acceptor (Jiang et al., 2021; Xiao et al., 2021). Because the change in the UV–vis absorption spectrum at 600 nm reflects the extent of DCPIP reduction, the DCPIP reduction assay provides an easily measurable visual output and is commonly used to estimate the activity of PS II (Fig. S7). Before conducting this experiment, we first determined whether the CDs are cytotoxic to plants, using thale cress (Arabidopsis thaliana) as a model organism. The chlorophyll fluorescence parameter showed that Arabidopsis thaliana (Athaliana) still photosynthesized well, with no obvious signs of inhibition after five days, when different concentrations of CDs were added to the hydroponic solution (Fig. S8 and S9). Measurement of photosynthetic activity then showed that, when chloroplasts/CDs were irradiated with simulated sunlight (intensity 20 mW cm−2), absorbance at 600 nm gradually decreased over time (Fig. S10), suggesting that photoreduction of DCPIP had occurred. The ratio of Δ absorbance to reaction time can be used to quantify and compare rates of photosynthetic activity of different samples. The rate of reduction of DCPIP by chloroplast/CDs complexes was markedly higher than that by chloroplasts alone. The cuticle layer of plants is composed of long-chain fatty acid polymers, containing hydroxyl and carboxyl groups (Baur et al., 1997; Xia et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2022). CDs can, therefore, enter Athaliana leaves through the micropores (exoplasm) of the cuticle layer on the surface of the leaves, which means that the effect of CDs on the growth of Athaliana can be investigated by spraying the leaves with a solution of CDs (Fig. 2c and 2d). The chlorophyll fluorescence parameter, variable fluorescence/maximum fluorescence (Fv/Fm), which is an indicator of the potential maximum photochemical efficiency of photosystem II, was measured after dark adaptation for 30 min. The Fv/Fm value in the presence of CDs was higher than that without CDs (Fig. 2e) and we found that Athaliana grows best when treated with 100 μg mL−1 CDs (Fig. 2f). The fresh weight of Athaliana leaves increased by 1.45-fold in the presence of 100 μg mL−1 CDs, confirming that our strategy not only works in vitro but also has exciting potential for enhancing photosynthesis in living plants.

To address potential safety concerns around spraying the leaves of edible plant crops with CDs, the CDs (20 μg mL−1 and 200 μg mL−1) were integrated into a PVA matrix to produce light-converting films (20-CDs@PVA and 200-CDs@PVA) (Fig. S11). Analysis of the XRD and FT-IR spectra of PVA, 20-CDs@PVA and 200-CDs@PVA revealed a decrease in the diffraction peak at 20° and an increase in the –C = N stretching vibration peak, respectively, indicating successful dispersion of CDs within the PVA matrix (Fig. S12 and 13). SEM images and elemental mapping confirmed the uniform dispersion of CDs within the PVA matrix (Fig. S14). The distribution of CDs ensures consistent optical properties across the film. The composite CDs@PVA films exhibited good optical transparency in the visible region and showed robust UV absorption (Fig. S15 and 16), influenced by the concentration of CDs. Higher concentrations can enhance absorption and emission but may reduce transmission due to increased scattering. The films were measured to have a thickness of approximately 75 μm (Table S1). Thicker films potentially have higher absorption due to the longer path length of light but may also result in decreased transmission. Additionally, we investigated the light density from 380 to 780 nm for both PVA and CDs@PVA films and found slight impact on the film's transparency with the addition of CDs (Table S1). However, the introduction of CDs did reduce the mechanical strength of the original PVA film (Fig. S17). This reduction in mechanical strength could be attributed to the disruption of the PVA polymer matrix by the embedded CDs. Based on these findings, we next investigated the optical properties of the CDs@PVAs films. Under UV and green irradiation, 20-CDs@PVA and 200-CDs@PVA produced blue fluorescence (centered at 445 nm) and red fluorescence (centered at 576 nm), respectively (Fig. 3 a). The emission spectra of CDs@PVA composite films are closely aligned with the absorbance spectra of chloroplasts within the range 400–570 nm and 530–750 nm (Fig. 3a), similar to the optical properties of CDs. Furthermore, these light conversion films exhibit excellent photostability (Fig. S18a and S18b). In contrast to CDs directly interacting with chloroplasts, CDs@PVA films primarily convert ultraviolet and green light from sunlight, which plants cannot fully utilize, into usable blue and red light. This enhances the plant's ability to capture sunlight and further boosts photosynthetic efficiency. Compared to CDs applied directly to plant surfaces, light-converting films (20-CDs@PVA and 200-CDs@PVA) highlight the potential of non-contact enhancement of natural photosynthesis.

We used the Hill reaction to examine the effect of CDs@PVA composite films on photosynthesis (Fig. 3b). 20-CDs@PVA and 200-CDs@PVA films can convert UV and green light from sunlight into blue and red light, respectively, which are usable by chloroplasts, and also have excellent light transmission properties. We next positioned pieces of 20-CDs@PVA and 200-CDs@PVA composite film in a quartz cuvette containing a mixture of chloroplasts and DCPIP and observed the time-dependent reduction of DCPIP when the mixture covered with the light-converting films was irradiated with simulated sunlight (intenisty, 20 mW cm−2) (Fig. 3c). We also carried out a control experiment, in which the reaction mixture was directly exposed to the light source without CDs@PVA films and an experiment in which the reaction mixture was covered with PVA film. As shown in Fig. 3d, the photosynthetic rate of the sample covered with the double-layer films was 0.175 min−1, which is an approximately 1.5-fold increase compared with the control film (0.116 min−1). DCPIP was reduced much more quickly in the presence of the composite films, indicating an enhanced rate of photosynthesis (Fig. 3d). These findings suggest that CDs@PVA composite films enhance photosynthesis by effectively harnessing both UV and green solar photons.

To prove this hypothesis, the 20-CDs@PVA and 200-CDs@PVA films were used separately to assess the relative contributions to the enhancement of photosynthesis under UV (intensity 15 mW cm−2) and green light (intensity 20 mW cm−2), respectively. As shown in Fig. S19, the rate of photosynthesis in the presence of the 20-CDs@PVA film was 0.096 min−1, which represents approximately 1.14-fold (0.084 min−1) and 2.26-fold (0.042 min−1) increases compared with PVA film and no film, respectively. A ferricyanide reduction assay was used to estimate the rate of electron transport between the CDs@PVA composite film and the chloroplasts. Compared with the control experiments with PVA film and no film, the 20-CDs@PVA film substantially increased the rate of electron transport (Fig. S20). Similarly, the rates of photosynthesis and ferricyanide reduction in the presence of 200-CDs@PVA were increased compared with PVA film and no film under green light (Fig. S21 and Fig. S22). These results prove that our hypothesis is correct, and that the double-photoconverting films can convert light that is unusable by chloroplasts into usable visible light, thus enhancing chloroplast photosynthesis. Light intensity is also a key factor that affects the rate of photosynthesis by chloroplasts. The rate of DCPIP reduction by chloroplasts covered with 20-CDs@PVA or 200-CDs@PVA film first increased and then decreased as the intensity of the UV or green light increased (Fig. S23 and Fig. S24). These results indicate that light conversion films can successfully convert non-absorbed light into visible light that chloroplasts can utilize within a specific range of light intensities. However, this enhancement does not increase indefinitely. We found that a light intensity of 20 mW cm−2 is optimal for both UV and green light, providing the best conditions for photosynthesis.

Encouraged by these in vitro results, we next investigated the effect of CDs@PVA composite films on living plants, using 2-week-old Athaliana plants as the model organism (Fig. 3b). The Athaliana plants were covered with double-layer films (20-CDs@PVA and 200-CDs@PVA) and then exposed to simulated sunlight (0.57 mW cm−2) for 1 h (Fig. 3e). The Fv/Fm value in the presence of the CDs@PVA composite films was ∼12% higher than without the film (Fig. 3f), confirming that our strategy also works in vivo and has huge potential for enhancing photosynthesis in living plants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism by which CDs@PVA films promote Athaliana photosynthesis
ce:section.ce:para: Increasing light harvesting capacity enhances the capture of photons, while improving electron transfer enhances the rate of reduction in photosynthetic electron transport, thereby enhancing the overall rate of photosynthesis. We further carried out transcriptome analysis on the leaves of Athaliana to investigate the potential molecular mechanism by which CDs@PVA films promote the growth of Athaliana. Clean reads from leaf samples grown in the presence and absence of CDs@PVA films were aligned with a reference genome sequence, where alignment efficiency was 93.24–97.25% (Table S2). The Q30 values of all samples were higher than 96.14% (sequencing error rate, 0.1%; Table S2). To screen the transcriptomic sequencing results for differentially expressed genes (DEGs), we used a threshold of fold change ≥ 2 (|log2FC| ≥1) and p-value < 0.05. Using these parameters, a total of 429 DEGs were identified: 82 upregulated genes and 347 downregulated genes (Fig. S25 and Table S3). GO enrichment analyses were then carried out to investigate the functionality of Athaliana genes that respond to light conversion films. These GO terms were associated with multiple biological processes, including 25 with biological processes, 15 with cellular components and 10 with molecular functions (Fig. 4 a and Table S4). To explore the physiological pathways implicated in the Athaliana response to light-converting films, we carried out GO enrichment analysis on the physiological processes linked to these DEGs (Fig. S26). The analysis revealed significant enrichment primarily in pathways such as cellular response to hypoxia (GO:0071456), DNA-binding transcription factor activity (GO:0003700), regulation of transcription, DNA-templated (GO:0006355), ethylene-activated signaling pathway (GO:0009873) and transcription cis-regulatory region binding (GO:0000976). Additionally, when we mapped the DEGs to reference canonical pathways in the KEGG database, we identified significant enrichment in pathways such as plant-pathogen interactions. This enrichment suggests potential mechanisms through which exposure to altered light spectra could influence the plant's immune system. One possible explanation is that such exposure may ‘prime’ the plant's defense mechanisms, leading to faster or stronger activation of defense mechanisms upon subsequent pathogen attack. This priming effect could result in increased expression of pathogen-related genes as a preemptive defense strategy. We also observed enrichment in pathways such as sesquiterpenoid and triterpenoid biosynthesis. This enrichment may be attributed to the plants increasing production of secondary metabolites in response to changes in light conditions. Some of these secondary metabolites have known roles in defense against pathogens, suggesting that the observed metabolic shift could lead to an increase in the expression of genes involved in the biosynthesis of these compounds. Furthermore, we found enrichment in pathways including the MAPK signaling pathway in plants, plant hormone signal transduction, and photosynthesis – antenna proteins (Fig. 4b). These results indicate that Athaliana regulates various biological processes and metabolic pathways to enhance photosynthesis when covered by light conversion films, providing assimilates and energy for the plants. Further analysis revealed that these genes are related to photosynthesis, especially the CAB3 (AT1G29910) and LHCB3 (AT5G54270) genes (Table S5), which play key roles in capturing and transferring light energy, providing photoprotection, dissipating excess energy, regulating the allocation of light energy between two photosystems and maintaining the structure of thylakoid membranes (Sattari Vayghan et al., 2022; Štroch et al., 2022). Compared with the control groups, genes that are upregulated in the presence of light conversion films are involved in ATP synthesis, rubisco enzyme activity, chlorophyll synthesis and photosystem I and II reactions. The broad modulation of biological processes, cellular components and molecular functions highlighted in this study underscores the potential of light conversion technology not only to boost photosynthetic efficiency but also to enhance overall plant health and productivity.

In conclusion, we have successfully synthesized carbon dots (CDs) with dual-emission properties using biomass-derived furfural. Application of solutions of the CDs onto Athaliana leaves for five days resulted in a 1.45-fold increase in leaf fresh weight, confirming the significant potential of these CDs for enhancing plant photosynthesis. To address potential safety concerns around spraying the leaves of edible plant crops with CDs, the CDs were integrated into a polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix to create dual light converting films (CDs@PVAs). These films have unique characteristics that enable efficient conversion of solar UV and green light—although not the primary wavelengths, they do have some impact on plant photosynthesis—into usable blue and red light, thereby significantly enhancing photosynthetic efficiency. Gene expression analysis showed that CDs@PVA films significantly enhance photosynthetic efficiency by upregulating genes involved in light-harvesting and energy conversion, notably CAB3 (AT1G29910) and LHCB3 (AT5G54270), which are crucial for capturing and transferring light energy. The new CDs@PVAs films represent a novel intelligent light converting film technology tailored for the agricultural sector and hold promise for a variety of applications. This innovation has the potential to inspire and provide practical solutions for enhancing crop growth efficiency, increasing yields and promoting environmental sustainability. The findings of this research will serve as valuable guidance and reference for future studies and practical implementation in related fields.

In future work, we plan to further refine the emission properties of the CDs to better align with chlorophyll absorption. This strategy includes exploring different dopants and fine-tuning the synthesis parameters to optimize the concentration and type of CDs. Additionally, we aim to modify the matrix materials to achieve better spectral overlap with chlorophyll and address the water resistance of the light-converting films. By advancing these aspects, we hope to enhance the practical application of this technology in agriculture, ensuring even greater improvements in crop growth efficiency and sustainability.

Min Ge: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Yue Yuan: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Shouxin Liu: Writing – review & editing. Jian Li: Writing – review & editing. Chenhui Yang: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Baosheng Du: Writing – review & editing. Qiuying Pang: Writing – review & editing. Shujun Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Zhijun Chen: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support for this research was furnished by the Key Research and Development Plan of National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 32171716, 31890774), the Heilongjiang Province (China) (Grant Nos. GZ20220115), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Grant No. 2021M690572), the Heilongjiang Provincial Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Grant No. LBHZ21033), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Grant No. 2572022BB05).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.ccst.2024.100253.
